Suggest measures to ensure the equitable adoption and effective utilization of ABDM services across urban and rural areas.
GS2.imageDescription: Abstract image showing digital health records on a tablet with medical symbols.
GS2.trivia: The Ayushman Bharat Yojana (PM-JAY) is the world's largest government-funded health assurance scheme.

GS2.headline: Outcomes of India-Central Asia Summit: Enhancing Connectivity and Regional Security
GS2.explainer:
• The recent India-Central Asia Summit concluded with renewed commitments to enhance connectivity, trade, and regional security cooperation.
• Leaders discussed projects related to the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) and Chabahar Port to boost trade.
• Emphasis was placed on counter-terrorism efforts, Afghanistan's stability, and cultural exchanges.
• India's 'Connect Central Asia' policy aims to strengthen ties with the five Central Asian republics.
• The summit highlighted India's growing strategic interest in the region for energy security and geopolitical balance.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests
prelimsPoints: Central Asian countries include Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan.
INSTC is a multi-modal transport route connecting India and Central Asia/Europe via Iran.
Chabahar Port in Iran is being developed by India to access Central Asia, bypassing Pakistan.
India is a member of the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO), which includes Central Asian states.
Energy security is a key driver for India's engagement with Central Asia.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of Central Asia for India's economic, energy, and security interests.
Discuss the various initiatives undertaken by India, such as INSTC and Chabahar Port, to enhance connectivity with Central Asia.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in strengthening India-Central Asia cooperation, particularly in trade and counter-terrorism.
Examine how India's 'Connect Central Asia' policy contributes to its broader foreign policy objectives and regional stability.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the International North-South Transport Corridor (INSTC) route.
GS2.trivia: The five Central Asian republics gained independence after the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.

GS3.headline: 27 Years of Pokhran-II: India's Nuclear Doctrine and Strategic Autonomy Reviewed
GS3.explainer:
• May 13, 1998, marked the completion of Operation Shakti, India's second series of nuclear tests at Pokhran, Rajasthan.
• The tests, including a thermonuclear device, cemented India's status as a nuclear weapons state.
• This anniversary prompts a review of India's 'No First Use' (NFU) policy and its implications for regional and global security.
• Discussions focus on the credibility of minimum credible deterrence and the evolving geopolitical landscape.
• The tests led to international sanctions, pushing India towards greater self-reliance in critical technologies.
gsSubTopics: Nuclear doctrine
India's security challenges
Science and Technology-indigenization
International relations - sanctions and strategic autonomy
prelimsPoints: Pokhran-II tests were conducted on May 11 and 13, 1998.
The operation was codenamed 'Operation Shakti'.
India declared itself a nuclear weapons state after these tests.
India's nuclear doctrine includes 'No First Use' and 'minimum credible deterrence'.
Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam played a crucial role in the Pokhran-II tests.
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolution and relevance of India's nuclear doctrine in the current geopolitical scenario.
Discuss how Pokhran-II influenced India's strategic autonomy and its approach to international relations.
Evaluate the effectiveness of 'minimum credible deterrence' as a security paradigm for India.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India's nuclear program in the context of global non-proliferation efforts.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of the Pokhran test site with a mushroom cloud in the background, symbolizing India's nuclear capability.
GS3.trivia: The scientists involved in Pokhran-II used code names for the tests, referring to different devices as 'Shakti-I', 'Shakti-II', etc.

GS3.headline: IMF Projects Strong Growth for India Amidst Global Economic Headwinds
GS3.explainer:
• The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has released its latest World Economic Outlook, projecting robust GDP growth for India in the coming fiscal year.
• This positive outlook is attributed to strong domestic demand, government capital expenditure, and ongoing structural reforms.
• Despite global inflation pressures and geopolitical uncertainties, India is expected to remain a bright spot in the world economy.
• Challenges include managing inflation, ensuring equitable growth, and boosting private investment.
• The report emphasizes the need for continued fiscal prudence and further reforms to sustain long-term growth momentum.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Effects of liberalization on the economy
prelimsPoints: IMF releases the World Economic Outlook report.
India is projected to be one of the fastest-growing major economies.
Key drivers include domestic demand and public investment.
Challenges include inflation and global economic slowdown.
IMF is an international financial institution headquartered in Washington, D.C.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's projected economic growth amidst global uncertainties.
Discuss the policy measures the Indian government can undertake to address inflation and ensure inclusive growth.
Evaluate the role of government capital expenditure in driving economic recovery and job creation.
Critically assess the challenges posed by global economic headwinds on India's growth trajectory and suggest mitigation strategies.
GS3.imageDescription: Graph depicting India's GDP growth trajectory compared to global average.
GS3.trivia: India is often referred to as a 'sweet spot' in the global economy due to its demographic dividend and strong consumption base.

GS3.headline: Government Reviews Progress of National Mission on Edible Oils – Oil Palm to Boost Domestic Production
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare conducted a review meeting on the National Mission on Edible Oils – Oil Palm (NMEO-OP).
• The mission aims to increase domestic production of palm oil and reduce India's reliance on imports, which currently stand at over 60% of consumption.
• Focus areas include increasing area under oil palm cultivation, improving productivity, and providing financial assistance to farmers.
• Environmental concerns regarding deforestation and biodiversity loss in traditional palm oil producing regions are being addressed through sustainable practices.
• The mission is crucial for enhancing farmers' income and achieving self-sufficiency in edible oils.
gsSubTopics: Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country
Agricultural produce and issues and related constraints
Food processing and related industries in India
Public Distribution System
keyTerms: National Mission on Edible Oils – Oil Palm (NMEO-OP)
prelimsPoints: NMEO-OP was launched with a special focus on the North-East region and the Andaman & Nicobar Islands.
India is the largest importer of edible oils globally.
Palm oil constitutes a significant portion of India's edible oil imports.
The mission aims to increase the area under oil palm cultivation to 10 lakh hectares by 2025-26.
It provides financial assistance to farmers for planting material and cultivation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of the National Mission on Edible Oils – Oil Palm for India's food security and economic stability.
Discuss the potential environmental and social impacts of expanding oil palm cultivation in India and measures for sustainable development.
Evaluate the challenges faced in achieving self-sufficiency in edible oils and suggest policy interventions to overcome them.
Examine how the NMEO-OP can contribute to increasing farmers' income and promoting agricultural diversification.
GS3.imageDescription: Oil palm plantation with rows of trees and ripe fruit bunches.
GS3.trivia: Palm oil is the most widely consumed vegetable oil in the world, accounting for about one-third of global vegetable oil production.

GS3.headline: Namami Gange Mission: Progress in River Cleaning and Conservation Initiatives
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti released a progress report on the Namami Gange Mission, highlighting achievements in wastewater treatment and solid waste management.
• Significant strides have been made in establishing Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) along the Ganges and its tributaries.
• Focus areas include industrial pollution abatement, rural sanitation, and biodiversity conservation in the river basin.
• The mission adopts a 'basin-wide approach' to rejuvenate the river, involving multiple stakeholders.
• Challenges persist in ensuring long-term sustainability, community participation, and addressing diffuse pollution sources.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster Management
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: Namami Gange Programme was launched in 2014.
It is an integrated conservation mission to clean and rejuvenate the Ganga river.
The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) is the implementation arm.
It focuses on pollution abatement, river front development, and biodiversity conservation.
It is under the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
mainsPoints: Critically evaluate the achievements and shortcomings of the Namami Gange Mission in achieving its objectives.
Discuss the multi-faceted approach adopted by the mission for Ganga rejuvenation and the role of various stakeholders.
Analyze the environmental, social, and economic impacts of river pollution and the importance of river conservation efforts.
Suggest innovative strategies and policy reforms to ensure the long-term sustainability and effectiveness of river cleaning initiatives in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Clean stretch of the Ganga river with ghats, symbolizing rejuvenation efforts.
GS3.trivia: The Ganga river basin is the most populous river basin in the world, supporting over 500 million people.

GS3.headline: Government Announces New Incentives to Boost Semiconductor Manufacturing in India
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian government has unveiled an enhanced incentive package to attract global semiconductor manufacturers and design firms.
• This move aims to establish India as a global hub for semiconductor production, crucial for electronics manufacturing and strategic autonomy.
• The new policy includes higher fiscal support, infrastructure development, and skill development programs.
• It seeks to address challenges like high capital intensity, technology transfer, and raw material sourcing.
• This initiative is vital for reducing import dependency and strengthening India's position in the global supply chain.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology- indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.

India aims to become a significant player in semiconductor manufacturing.
The Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme is a key government initiative.
Challenges include high capital investment and complex technology.
Semiconductor chips are made primarily from silicon.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of developing a robust semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem in India for economic growth and national security.
Discuss the key features of the government's incentive schemes for semiconductor production and their potential impact.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in attracting foreign investment and technology transfer in the semiconductor sector.
Suggest policy measures to build a sustainable and competitive semiconductor industry, including skill development and R&D.
GS3.imageDescription: Close-up of a silicon wafer with intricate circuit patterns.
GS3.trivia: A single modern smartphone can contain billions of transistors, each smaller than a human hair, fabricated on a semiconductor chip.

GS3.headline: Coastal States Intensify Mangrove Conservation Efforts to Combat Climate Change
GS3.explainer:
• Several coastal states in India, including West Bengal, Gujarat, and Andhra Pradesh, are ramping up efforts to conserve and restore mangrove ecosystems.
• Mangroves act as natural barriers against cyclones and tsunamis, protect coastlines from erosion, and serve as vital carbon sinks.
• Initiatives include large-scale mangrove plantation drives, community participation programs, and strict enforcement against encroachment.
• The conservation efforts are crucial for enhancing climate resilience and protecting unique biodiversity.
• Challenges include rising sea levels, industrial pollution, and unsustainable aquaculture practices.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster Management
Biodiversity
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: Mangroves are salt-tolerant plant communities found in tropical and subtropical intertidal zones.
They are crucial for coastal protection and act as nurseries for marine life.
India has significant mangrove cover, with the Sundarbans being the largest single block.
Mangroves are highly effective carbon sinks, storing more carbon than terrestrial forests.
The 'Mangrove Initiative for Shoreline Habitats & Tangible Incomes (MISHTI)' scheme promotes mangrove plantation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological and economic significance of mangrove ecosystems for India's coastal regions.
Analyze the threats faced by mangroves due to climate change, human activities, and developmental projects.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current government and community-led initiatives in mangrove conservation and restoration.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for sustainable management of mangrove ecosystems, integrating policy, community participation, and scientific research.
GS3.imageDescription: Dense mangrove forest with roots visible in the water, showcasing their unique adaptation.
GS3.trivia: The pneumatophores (breathing roots) of mangroves help them to respire in oxygen-deficient marshy soils.

GS3.headline: Scientists Discover New Species in Western Ghats, Highlighting Biodiversity Hotspot Status
GS3.explainer:
• A team of researchers has announced the discovery of a new species of amphibian/plant/insect in the biodiversity-rich Western Ghats.
• This discovery underscores the critical importance of the Western Ghats as a global biodiversity hotspot facing increasing environmental threats.
• The new species adds to the already extensive list of endemic flora and fauna found in the region.
• Conservation efforts are vital to protect these unique ecosystems from habitat loss, climate change, and pollution.
• The discovery highlights the need for continued exploration and scientific documentation of India's natural heritage.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation
Biodiversity
Environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: The Western Ghats is one of the 36 global biodiversity hotspots.
It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
It is home to a high degree of endemism, particularly in amphibians and reptiles.
Threats include deforestation, mining, and climate change.
The region is known for its shola forests and grasslands.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ecological significance of the Western Ghats as a biodiversity hotspot and the factors contributing to its unique species richness.
Analyze the major threats to biodiversity in the Western Ghats and the consequences of habitat loss.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current conservation strategies in the region and suggest measures for enhanced protection.
Examine the role of scientific research and community participation in identifying and conserving new species and their habitats.
GS3.imageDescription: Close-up photo of a newly discovered species of frog/flower from the Western Ghats.
GS3.trivia: The Western Ghats are older than the Himalayan mountains and influence the Indian monsoon weather patterns.

GS3.headline: Government Proposes New Urban Flood Management Policy Amidst Increasing Extreme Weather Events
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs has drafted a comprehensive national policy for urban flood management.
• The policy aims to integrate early warning systems, sustainable drainage solutions, and land-use planning to mitigate flood risks.
• It emphasizes community participation, capacity building of urban local bodies, and climate-resilient infrastructure development.
• The move comes in response to recurrent urban flooding incidents exacerbated by climate change and unplanned urbanization.
• Effective implementation requires coordination among multiple agencies and significant investment in infrastructure.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation
prelimsPoints: Urban flooding is a major challenge in Indian cities, especially during monsoon.
Causes include rapid urbanization, encroachment on natural drainage, and inadequate infrastructure.
National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) provides guidelines for urban flood management.
Early warning systems are crucial for disaster preparedness.
Green infrastructure, like permeable pavements and rain gardens, can help manage stormwater.
mainsPoints: Analyze the primary causes and consequences of urban flooding in India, considering both climatic and anthropogenic factors.
Discuss the key features of the proposed national urban flood management policy and its potential to enhance urban resilience.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing effective urban flood management strategies, including inter-agency coordination and funding.
Suggest innovative and sustainable solutions for urban flood mitigation, focusing on integrated water management and smart city concepts.
GS3.imageDescription: Overhead view of a flooded urban street with vehicles partially submerged.
GS3.trivia: Mumbai's Mithi River, once a clean waterway, became a major source of urban flooding due to encroachment and pollution.

GS3.headline: Indian Railways Accelerates Modernization Drive with Focus on High-Speed Corridors and Freight Logistics
GS3.explainer:
• Indian Railways is pushing forward with its ambitious modernization plan, including the development of high-speed rail corridors.
• The focus is on upgrading infrastructure, enhancing passenger experience, and improving freight logistics efficiency.
• Projects include the Bullet Train network, dedicated freight corridors (DFCs), and electrification of railway lines.
• The modernization aims to reduce travel time, boost economic activity, and reduce carbon footprint.
• Challenges involve land acquisition, funding, and integrating advanced technologies while maintaining safety standards.

Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology- indigenization of technology
prelimsPoints: Indian Railways is the fourth largest railway network in the world by size.
The first passenger train ran in India in 1853 (Mumbai to Thane).
Dedicated Freight Corridors (Eastern and Western) aim to de-congest existing lines.
The Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail project is India's first Bullet Train project.
'Vande Bharat Express' trains are indigenous semi-high-speed trains.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-dimensional impact of Indian Railways' modernization drive on the country's economy, logistics, and regional connectivity.
Discuss the strategic importance of high-speed rail corridors and dedicated freight corridors for India's future development.

Examine how technological advancements and private sector participation can accelerate the transformation of the Indian railway system.
GS3.imageDescription: Modern Vande Bharat Express train speeding on tracks.
GS3.trivia: The world's highest railway bridge, Chenab Bridge, is part of the Udhampur-Srinagar-Baramulla Rail Link project in Jammu and Kashmir.

GS3.headline: ISRO Enhances Capabilities for Space Debris Tracking and Mitigation
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is investing in advanced technologies for tracking and mitigating space debris.
• This initiative is critical as the increasing number of satellite launches raises concerns about orbital congestion and collision risks.
• ISRO's efforts include developing indigenous radar systems, optical telescopes, and software for space situational awareness.
• India is also advocating for international cooperation and adherence to guidelines for sustainable space operations.
• Effective debris management is vital for the long-term safety and sustainability of India's space assets and future missions.
gsSubTopics: Awareness in the fields of Space
Science and Technology- indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Challenges to internal security through communication networks
prelimsPoints: Space debris includes defunct satellites, spent rocket stages, and fragments from collisions.
Project NETRA (Network for Space Object Tracking and Analysis) is ISRO's initiative for Space Situational Awareness.
LEO (Low Earth Orbit) is particularly susceptible to orbital congestion.
Kessler Syndrome describes a scenario where the density of objects in LEO is high enough that collisions generate more debris.
ISRO follows international guidelines for debris mitigation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing threat of space debris to active satellites and future space missions, globally and for India.
Analyze ISRO's initiatives and technological advancements in tracking and mitigating space debris.
Evaluate the challenges in managing space debris, including the need for international cooperation and policy frameworks.
Suggest measures for promoting sustainable space practices and ensuring the long-term viability of outer space activities.
GS3.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of Earth's orbit filled with numerous pieces of space debris.
GS3.trivia: The first piece of space debris was the satellite Sputnik 1's upper stage, which remained in orbit for over two months after launch in 1957.

GS3.headline: Addressing Agrarian Distress: Government Explores New Policy Interventions for Farmers' Welfare
GS3.explainer:
• The government is reportedly considering new policy measures to address persistent agrarian distress, particularly for small and marginal farmers.
• Discussions revolve around enhancing crop insurance schemes, improving market access, and providing direct income support.
• Focus areas include reducing input costs, promoting crop diversification, and strengthening agricultural infrastructure.
• Climate change impacts, fluctuating market prices, and debt burden remain significant challenges for the agricultural sector.
• The aim is to ensure sustainable livelihoods for farmers and bolster food security for the nation.

Agricultural produce and issues and related constraints

Public Distribution System

MSP is a form of market intervention by the government to protect farmers.
Agricultural credit is provided through various financial institutions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted causes of agrarian distress in India and its implications for rural livelihoods and national food security.
Discuss the effectiveness of existing government schemes and policy interventions in addressing farmers' challenges.
Evaluate the potential impact of new policy measures, such as enhanced crop insurance or direct income support, on agricultural sustainability.
Suggest comprehensive reforms in agricultural marketing, credit, and infrastructure to ensure long-term farmers' welfare and reduce distress.
GS3.imageDescription: Farmer working in a field with modern agricultural machinery.

GS3.headline: India's Progress in Indigenous Vaccine Development and Global Health Diplomacy
GS3.explainer:
• India continues to make significant strides in indigenous vaccine development, building on lessons from the recent pandemic.
• Research and development efforts are focused on new pathogens, tropical diseases, and improving vaccine delivery mechanisms.
• India's 'Vaccine Maitri' initiative has positioned it as a key player in global health diplomacy, supplying vaccines to many nations.
• The emphasis is on strengthening public-private partnerships and enhancing manufacturing capacities.
• This self-reliance in vaccine production is crucial for national health security and global equitable access to healthcare.

Biotechnology is a key sector for India's scientific and economic growth.

GS3.imageDescription: Vials of vaccines in a laboratory setting, symbolizing research and production.

GS3.headline: India Crosses Major Milestone in Solar Energy Capacity Addition, Eyes Ambitious 2030 Targets
GS3.explainer:
• India has achieved a significant milestone in its installed solar energy capacity, pushing towards its ambitious renewable energy targets.
• The growth is driven by government policies like the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar PV manufacturing and solar parks.
• This expansion is crucial for energy security, reducing carbon emissions, and meeting international climate commitments.
• Challenges include land availability, grid integration, and dependence on imported components for solar cells and modules.
• The focus is now on energy storage solutions and promoting decentralized solar applications.
